title: Half-Wave Rectifier Circuit
description: Converts AC to pulsating DC using a single diode. Only positive half-cycles of the AC input pass to the load.
theory: A half-wave rectifier is the simplest form of a rectifier circuit that converts alternating current (AC) to direct current (DC). It uses a single PN junction diode that conducts only during the positive half-cycle of the AC input signal. During the positive half-cycle, the diode is forward-biased and current flows through the load resistor R_L. During the negative half-cycle, the diode is reverse-biased and no current flows. As a result, the output is a series of positive pulses separated by zero intervals, making the output a pulsating DC waveform. The output frequency equals the input supply frequency. The average DC output voltage is approximately 0.318 × Vm (where Vm is the peak voltage), and the RMS output is 0.5 × Vm. The rectification efficiency is 40.6% and the ripple factor is 1.21, indicating significant ripple which typically requires filtering for practical use.
keyPoints: Only one diode is used — the simplest and cheapest rectifier configuration.
Conducts only during the POSITIVE half-cycle of AC input; negative half-cycle is blocked.
Average DC output voltage = 0.318 × Vm (Vm = peak AC voltage).
Rectification efficiency = 40.6% — lowest among rectifier types.
Ripple factor = 1.21 — high ripple; a filter capacitor is usually required.
Ripple frequency equals supply frequency (e.g., 50 Hz input → 50 Hz ripple).
PIV (Peak Inverse Voltage) rating of the diode must exceed Vm to prevent breakdown.
Form factor = Vrms / Vdc = 1.57; transformer utilization factor (TUF) = 0.287.
examTip: Half-wave rectifier conducts only during POSITIVE half-cycle of AC input. Output DC voltage = 0.318 × Vm (Vm = peak voltage). Efficiency = 40.6%. Ripple factor = 1.21. Frequency of ripple = supply frequency (50Hz → 50Hz ripple). Uses only 1 diode — simplest rectifier. PIV (Peak Inverse Voltage) of diode must exceed Vm.
useCases: Simple, low-cost power supplies where DC quality is not critical.
Low-power applications where efficiency is not the primary concern.
Educational/laboratory demonstrations of basic rectification principles.

wires.comment: AC source top terminal up to top-left corner
Top rail, left — approaching diode anode
Top rail, right — diode cathode to RL top
RL top lead down to RL component
RL bottom lead to bottom rail
AC source bottom terminal down to bottom rail
Bottom return rail — completing the loop
